sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Dialysis emergencies can include circuit disconnection, access haemorrhage, air embolism, severe hypotension, dysrhythmia, sepsis, and electrolyte complications. Prehospital priorities are scene-safe machine separation where required, bleeding control, airway and seizure management, and rapid transport.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Collapse, chest pain, dyspnoea, seizure, stroke-like deficit, or reduced conscious state during or soon after dialysis
- Significant bleeding from a fistula, graft, or dialysis tubing
- Hypotension, pallor, tachycardia, or shock
- Hyperkalaemia features such as weakness, bradycardia, or arrhythmia
- Fever, rigors, or abdominal pain suggesting infection, especially in peritoneal dialysis patients

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- If the patient is still connected, disconnection and clamping may be time critical but should be done only by people who can do so safely
- Access sites may bleed heavily once disconnected
- Air embolism can present with acute neurological or cardiorespiratory collapse
- Avoid using the dialysis access limb for routine IV procedures unless there is no alternative and the situation is life threatening

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- If still connected to the dialysis circuit, stop the machine and clamp lines only if this can be done safely and promptly
- Control access-site bleeding with direct pressure and escalate to tourniquet use only if severe haemorrhage cannot be controlled otherwise
- Give oxygen only if clinically indicated and support ventilation if required
- Manage seizures or altered conscious state under the relevant emergency pathway
- Prioritise urgent transport for collapse, neurological deficit, severe bleeding, or suspected hyperkalaemia

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG
- Gain IV access away from the dialysis limb if possible
- Consider crystalloid therapy for symptomatic hypotension or shock, with repeated reassessment
- Treat hyperkalaemia, seizure, or severe haemorrhage according to the relevant protocol and companion guideline
- Consider prehospital blood sampling only if this does not delay time-critical transport
- Pre-notify early when air embolism, major haemorrhage, hyperkalaemia, or sepsis is suspected

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Peritoneal dialysis patients with abdominal pain, cloudy fluid, or fever should raise concern for infection or sepsis
- Document the dialysis setting, whether the machine was disconnected, access-site management, and any suspected air entry into the circuit
- Patients may know the name of their dialysis centre or machine and this information may help hospital teams
